Transcrição Completa: 87 Case Study Investing for Faster Sales Cycle 6 15 24     [00:00] Okay, welcome in. So, on this week's weekly group call, I had a really good case study this week that I wanted to talk with you guys about. We've been working with a client recently who had a tremendously long sales cycle.   [00:15] They were raising capital for an upcoming project that they had and I found it quite interesting in terms of what we deployed on this specific project and how well this client worked with us on this.   [00:28] Shortened their sales cycle from about 9 to 16 weeks on average down to about three weeks, which in the raising capital space for the amount of money that they raise per person is tremendous in terms of the time crunch.   [00:43] To be clear, I then rolled these same principles out to clients that I consider willing and able to actually implement what I'm going to talk to you guys about today.   [00:53] It's very important to note on that same related topic, not every person, not every business is actually willing to be as transparent and willing to build as much trust as what they need to in order to condense their sales cycle down.   [01:08] Not every business is willing to just simply put in the effort either in order to shorten their sales cycle down.   [01:16] Um, it really depends on once again how willing and able you are to actually implement a lot of what we're going to talk about today. So, just quick disclaimer, if you're about the action, this will work tremendously well for you.   [01:30] Um, to be clear, this particular client, they're raising capital for car washes. So, they go essentially location to location in all these different places.   [01:40] They do these like self-service car washes that have a hands-on component as well as kind of like an upsell, like a detailing service.   [01:49] And on average for each one of the projects that they have, they look to raise about $2 to $5 million depending on where the location is.   [01:58] The average person that they need to work with them is an accredited investor. First of all, second of all, the minimum amount of money that they work with somebody on is $75,000.   [02:09] So that's the minimum that somebody has to have in order to be qualified accredited and 75K. They can have up to about a million dollars.   [02:18] Once they exceed a million dollars in investment per person, that individual investor becomes a personal guarantor, which means that, god forbid, in the instance that that business specifically fails and an individual invested essentially more than 20% of the value of the project, they would be personally on the line in the instance that the project fails.   [02:40] So, moral of the story, we tried to keep for this particular client the total investable amount to less than a million dollars. So, that'd be the ceiling of what somebody would be considered qualified.   [02:52] Now, to be clear, what's really cool with this particular client, about 26% of their leads come through having $700,000 to a million dollar able to invest, which is awesome.   [03:05] Now, to be clear, those people typically have a really long sales cycle, just to put it in perspective.   [03:11] Uh, you know, they don't know this company. They're asking for, you know, as low as, once again, $75,000, which is still a very respectable amount of money, all the way up to a million dollars. Okay?   [03:23] The common investor that's accredited, that's shopping and doing their due diligence, they have all kinds of shit that they look for and that they research about individuals and the organizations that they're investing into, past projects, current projects.   [03:37] Uh there's there's a there's a lot. Simply put, there's just a lot. And a lot of that due diligence has to be compressed with the company providing a lot of that research that the individual investor is looking for in order to speed up the sales cycle.   [03:52] Now, this set of lessons I'm about to go through, this is applicable once again for any kind of offer, any kind of business because the same general lessons apply.   [04:02] If you have a longer sales cycle, once again, and you want to shorten it down, you have to follow these general principles.   [04:09]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Speed to Trust Equation"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados numericamente   VISUAL:  Conceito visual mostrando a relação entre confiança e velocidade de vendas, equação de Steven Covey   [04:11] So number one, the most important thing to understand in terms of what speeds up any kind of deal getting done is one word, trust. Okay, there's a really good book by a guy named Steven Covey.   [04:23] Steven Covey has a book called Speed to Trust. And simply put, a lot of these general frames that we're going to talk through will originate from that book, just to be clear if you guys want to read through it.   [04:34] And the concept is really simple. I think in the first like five pages, he lists out an equation. It's very simple. The higher the trust, the faster everything goes in terms of speed. Okay?   [04:45] The lower the trust, the slower everything goes.   [04:49] And so a lot of people, you know, they'll they'll get a lead and they'll expect the person to just kind of come through well-framed already because they did something as simple as like book a call or express interest, you know.   [05:02] And for those who aren't familiar, I talk through this a lot inside of the sales and connection rate mastery document that I have where we talk about the interest levels of a lead versus the interest levels of a salesperson and just where those levels need to be at in order for a deal to get done.   [05:19]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Interest Levels: Lead vs Salesperson"   NÚMEROS:  Níveis de interesse representados visualmente (curious → convicted)   VISUAL:  Escala de interesse mostrando progressão de "curious" (curioso) para "high conviction" (alta convicção), comparação entre lead e vendedor   [05:21] Everybody knows that obviously a deal gets done when both people, both the salesperson and the lead themselves are at a state of high conviction.   [05:29] And the salesperson needs to stay at a state and start at a state above or at least at this level of high conviction.   [05:36] A lead when they come in, they're typically just curious. And this is what a lot of sales organizations have a big problem with and and what a lot of marketers need to acknowledge so they can help out in this process.   [05:49] The sales people will talk about the people who come in just curious as cold. Like they'll say things like, "Oh, these people aren't ready to buy yet."   [05:58] Uh you know the and and what they're doing is is simply put they're comparing it to people that in the same example are at simply put just a higher level of interest already.   [06:08] So they might get somebody that comes through that's you know like an organic lead is typically what they compare it to where they say people are hotter and all the big difference is is you know when you look at organic leads versus you look at paid leads or advertising based leads that they might come through simply put just a higher level of interest because they've already consumed you know x amount of pieces of content and they're closer simply put in terms of stages of of conviction rather than being down here at curious you know they're already upwards of you one step away from conviction, high conviction where the deal gets done.   [06:44] So, simply put, if you look at this a little differently in comparison to how, you know, I framed it inside of the sales rate, the sales connection and show rate mastery document.   [06:54] You know, we we want to step away from a salesperson saying, "Okay, these leads that come through just curious are cold." Okay? We want to step away from that specific word being used at all.   [07:05]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Cold = Low Trust"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados numericamente   VISUAL:  Reframing mental: leads "frios" são na verdade leads de "baixa confiança", mudança de perspectiva visual   [07:07] We'd rather say that this type of lead is actually just low trust. Okay. When we look at it from that perspective, both as salespeople and marketers, what we can do to control the process to get them at a higher level of conviction sooner changes dramatically when we just rephrase it to, okay, they're not cold, they're low trust.   [07:27] So simply put once again low trust equals cold. Low trust also equals slow. Okay?   [07:34] These are the people who have the longest steps, the longest amount of time in each one of our sales cycles.   [07:40] Does this make sense so far to you guys? Anybody? Hello? Okay. Just think of it as simple as well what does that what does that simply mean you know low trust it's low trust how do we create trust so I like to use California as an example one huge difference between the east coast and California specifically is social networking to get a deal done in New York or really anywhere on the east coast from my experience if I pitch somebody who has no idea who I them and it just makes sense to them, there's an extremely high probability that they'll pull the trigger.   [15:02] They won't reach out to a bunch of people that potentially know me and ask about me, you know, even though they'll hear great things. To be clear, they'll just pull the trigger.   [15:11] It's a very different world in California from what I've experienced in California. It's like if I get introduced to somebody, one of the very very first things before you even talk about doing a deal, just one of the very first things that somebody will typically talk about with you is just who you know.   [15:28] And then what they're trying to do is they're trying to find mutual connections because there's a really interesting paradigm that happens in California.   [15:36] There's really good quality people and at the same time there are some of the worst fucking people on planet Earth that reside in California and they're extremely low trust.   [15:46] they're fake, you know, they they're like low status and they like just pretend everything. Uh even worse, you know, they're like legitimate scammers or they just simply put have low integrity and do business deals poorly. You know, they fuck people over in the past.   [16:02] Now, word spreads around very quickly in in in an area where this kind of social dynamic exists, okay?   [16:10]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "West Coast Social Vetting Process"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados numericamente   VISUAL:  Processo de verificação social da Califórnia: encontrar conexões mútuas, law of association, vetting imediato   [16:12] where one of the first things that people will do is just simply put try to see if you know people that they know that are credible without even having to reach out and ask them just pure law of association. Okay, you immediately get vetted from that perspective.   [16:26]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Solution #1: Historical Investor Rolodex"   NÚMEROS:  152 historical investors contacted, 90+ agreed to be listed   VISUAL:  Estratégia de rolodex de investidores, permissão para revelar nomes, social proof através de conexões reais   [16:28] So here's one of the first things that we implemented for this specific company based on that logic is we made every investor from those 151 I'm sorry I believe it was 152 152 historical investors we made sure to reach out to each one of those individual investors and just ask a simple question.   [16:48] Hey, if we have somebody who's on the fence with investing with us, are you okay that we reveal that you've invested with us historically and have generated you a return?   [16:58] Now, keep in mind 151 out of 152 of those historical investors have reinvested. Out of the 151 that have successfully reinvested, we got about 90ish people. I don't know the exact number. It was somewhere between like 90 and 95.   [17:13] It might have grown since to be fair because this is still an ongoing thing, but 90ish people out of the 151 said, "Yeah, man. Throw my name out there. Let people know that I invested with you guys. I'm happy to do that. You guys have done great work."   [17:28]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Rolodex Implementation"   NÚMEROS:  90+ investors listed   VISUAL:  Formato de rolodex: First name, Last name, Investment project, Return, City/State - NO contact info   [17:30] Now, so here's one of the first things that we do in the initial emails that go out to people and also in the initial conversations that happen. We share a rolodex of other investors and very simple information.   [17:42] First name, last name, what they invested into, what their return was, and specifically what city and state they are in.   [17:50] Didn't share contact information like email or phone number. That's unnecessary. Simply put, we just wanted people who came through this lead list to be able to see a list of 90 plus people that have invested in the past and just be able to simply scan through the list and say, "Oh yeah, I know that guy." Or, "Oh, yeah, I know that girl."   [18:10] And believe it or not, this one thing did such a tremendous job at shortening the sales cycle because it immediately implemented the tactic of social networking to build trust.   [18:22] If I know people that you know and I know that those people are credible or I just know of those people and I know that they're legitimate, that alone increases the trust dramatically.   [18:33] So for this particular organization, just being transparent about the people that invested with them, dramatically increase the trust that would happen right away. It would advance the conversation.   [18:45] there were still a lot of people who would have no idea of anybody on that list and it didn't really do that much for those specific people. It acted as social proof but it didn't really do a tremendous job.   [18:57]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Case Study Videos"   NÚMEROS:  20+ video interviews, 15% play rate   VISUAL:  Página com 20 entrevistas em vídeo de investidores históricos, estatísticas de visualização do Wistia   [18:59] It's the same thing with case studies. We implemented about 20-ish videos. There's an entire page dedicated with interviews from 20 of these 151 plus historical investors that are just interviews talking about their investments and what their return was like, what their experience was like working with the organization.   [19:18] And for the people who are doing their due diligence, this helps a lot. It helps a lot to help the person do their research.   [19:25] But at the same time, this is the experience of 20 people out of 152. So, does that really land extremely well in building trust rapidly? Yeah. I mean, it helps for some people that are highly analytical and willing to watch 20 plus videos for sure because they're great videos.   [19:43] But what about the guys? What? Answer this question. Put in the chat. Would you watch 20 plus videos from these individual investors historically? Are you that type of person? Or would you just look at the fact there are 20 videos and just say, "Oh, cool."   [19:59] A lot of people don't watch them. We look at that based on the Wistia statistics of each one of those videos. We can see the total quantity of people that hit the page versus the total quantity of people that pressed play.   [20:11] We can also see the individual playthrough rates in terms of how long somebody watched each one of the videos.   [20:17] There's about a 15% total play rate, give or take the day, a little more, a little less, and give or take the video. A little more, a little less. But about 15% of the people that just hit the page, press play on one of those videos.   [20:31] So, what's that go to show? Well, that goes to show most of these people will click on the page. They'll look at it, but they don't actually consume that data. They just interpret the fact that there's 20 plus case studies.   [20:43] So, that can't be considered highly effective when it comes to building trust. It's an element that builds trust, but there's two general things that we want to consider when we're building trust in our in our sales process to speed up the sales cycle.   [20:58]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "High Impact vs Low Impact Actions"   NÚMEROS:  1-2% actions vs 10-20% actions   VISUAL:  Comparação visual entre ações de baixo impacto (1-2% trust) e alto impacto (10-20% trust), estratégia de priorização   [21:00] What is a high impact action and what is a low impact action? We still want to take the low impact actions. Okay? I'm not encouraging you ignore low impact actions. Just want to be very very clear on that.   [21:12] But what we want to look for is we want to look for those high impact actions. That way we can speed up that cycle tremendously because keep in mind if we've got to incrementally take somebody's trust and we're using little building blocks that build trust like you know one to 2% at a time that's not bad.   [21:28] you know, like we want to do those things, but we want to make sure we also incorporate those things that add a good like 10 to maybe 20% amount of trust rather than just a little like 1 to 2% increment in comparison. Does that make sense?   [21:42] So, to be clear, how do we know what is a 1 to 2% action and what is a 10 to 20% action? That then becomes the question.   [21:50] Now, to be fair, 1 to 2% actions, you know, if I take 10 1% actions, that obviously equates to 10%. That's why we still want to take them. Okay.   [22:00] But remember some of the most obvious things we have to be very very very particular about. And when we think about it from this perspective of okay, how can I increase trust the fastest so I can speed everything up? I started coming up with different ideas. Okay.   [22:15] So like I said, I considered the west coast way of doing deals versus the east coast way of doing deals. West Coast way of doing deals. Once again, one of the highest impact actions that we had taken at one of those 10% actions was the rolodex of historical investors.   [22:30] So, I ask you a simple question for your offers. If you had to create a rolodex that people could go through your potential leads that want to become buyers to increase trust faster, how many of your total buyers would be willing to put their name on that list?   [22:46] You know, one of the number one reasons that people will join into the inner circle is they already know somebody that's in the inner circle that's getting an incredible result with it and they just like being a part of it and they go and they tell their friends about it and then somebody will come and apply to the inner circle and they'll be like, "Oh yeah, I know like so and so that's a part of the group, man. They say they love that shit so you know, I wanted to join in, too."   [23:10] And these are some of the easiest people to close. Why? Well, because they already know somebody that's high trust and that trust transfers. Okay.   [23:20] Case study videos, you know, doesn't have the same impact, does it? Okay. So, to be clear, would you create a rolodex for your specific offer and how would you implement it?   [23:31] For us, it was just a long fucking table that we'd put inside of an email and send to an individual.   [23:37] Now, some of us, like me, as an example, like I have 4,000 paid students that have learned how to start and scale marketing agencies or digital marketing lessons. I can't put 4,000 people in an email, you know, that's just uh it's not it's not going to be how it goes.   [23:53] But, you know, to be clear, one thing that we're working on is a literal dedicated page on our website with just pages and pages and pages and pages of people you can scroll through alphabetically and also you can search through and literally the page is going to be framed for it's going to say search for your friends, see who's already per purchased with us and ask them what their experience was and then it's going to have a search function.   [24:17] It's literally just going to have the ability to look through everybody who's purchased with us historically.   [24:22] Now, to be clear, think about how this could be taken advantage of. Okay? There's some skepticism that you have to consider. Like, if I'm willing to put my entire buyer list out there on a web page, could that be leveraged against me somehow by like a competitor? I don't know. Maybe.   [24:38] Now, do I give a fuck about that? No. No. You know why? Because when we do things that create trust faster, there's an equal probability that we can think with the downside of, well, how could this fuck me?   [24:51] You know, it's like relationships. If I go into a relationship with a new partner, somebody that I might want to marry one day, guess what? There's a lot of ways that person can fuck you, but there's a lot of ways that things could go right as well.   [25:04] So, if you enter into that relationship thinking to yourself the 50/50 odds of like, well, things could go great or things could go poorly, there's a low amount of trust that gets established right away because you're kind of one foot in and one foot out versus if you go fully committed into the relationship right away and the other counterparty does the same thing.   [25:23] There's a lot of benefit to that speed to advancing in the relationship faster occurs because both parties entered into it with a high state of trust.   [25:32] So, the moral of the story is we also want to make sure that when we take these actions, we're not just thinking with how could this fuck me. Okay, remember we're trying to think with the upside of if I was doing everything from a perspective of I'm just trying to increase trust as fast as I can and I was willing to do that. That's what I'm going to do.   [25:51] You know, in the instances that some of these things could backfire in some kind of negative way, think with them for sure, but don't don't act with them. You know, just kind of be mindful of them.   [26:01] a rolodex of your clientele that doesn't have phone numbers and emails can't even be used to create a list. You know, it's very simple. It's just names, just people searching for their friends.   [26:11] And then the intention of this once again is if they see one of their friends, they can go ask that individual, hey, what was your experience like?   [26:18] And then remember, a high trust relationship that already exists transfers that trust into us once they know that there's somebody else out there that already has a great relationship with us, that's already trusting, and that they've already benefited from. simply put.   [26:33] So, we want to leverage that West Coast social dynamic inside of our sales processes. Okay.   [26:39]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Solution #2: The Research Deck"   NÚMEROS:  21 total projects, all financials included   VISUAL:  Deck abrangente de due diligence: projetos atuais, históricos, detalhes de pessoas, background checks, links de redes sociais   [26:41] Next variable that we added. So, obviously in this investor's case, you know, when somebody's considering putting up money for a car wash, one of the one of the dynamics that exists is the due diligence of well, how how and why does it make sense for me to put my money here?   [26:58] You know, like what have past projects looked like? Uh, you know, what what's his current project look like? like they've obviously got to be sold on the investment at the same time. Can't just be based on trust and social dynamics alone.   [27:11] So the obvious things are some of those I'd consider them still one to 2% actions. Some of them to be fair might be like 3 to 5% actions.   [27:20] But in this case we call it a a deck. So we put together just a research deck. And this research deck has a fuckload of information inside of it.   [27:29] It has current project details. It has historical project details and more importantly than anything else, it has details on the people that are running the project.   [27:40] Every single person that's involved. It has links to their background checks. Okay? It has links to all of their social media sites so they can be researched.   [27:50] It has links to every individual personal detail that we could find that was publicly available for somebody to research.   [27:58] uh we put instead of photos of the person, we put photos of these individuals with their families, okay, the people who run the projects.   [28:07] How many people do you know with children that say things like, "Well, I'm not putting my children on social media. I don't want them to be like trained on AI, you know, or I don't want them to I don't want people to know who they are."   [28:20] How many times have you found a person that's like, "Yeah, I don't want to put my face on this project, you know? I want to do like a faceless fucking marketing campaign, you know?   [28:29] You know what I think right away? Every time we get a potential client that says like, "Oh yeah, I don't want to put my face out here." Half of them, I should say more than half of them, aren't even focused on the fact they could potentially sell their organizations.   [28:44] They're just focused on the fact that they don't necessarily believe in their offers enough, they view whatever they're marketing and selling as a as something that could backfire on them in the future, and so they don't want to associate their personal names with it.   [28:59] Not in this investor's case. these guys who are raising money. Once again, photos of them and their families, fullon fucking bios about them as individuals.   [29:09] Like I said, links to their actual fucking background checks so you can check out the criminal history on these individuals.   [29:17] You know, it's amazing amazing credibility that happens when you take these extra steps.   [29:23] Because keep in mind most people would at the surface level just put together a informative deck about the current project and maybe a handful of past projects.   [29:33] These guys in total have done about 21 individual car washes in the past. Should we hand select a few of the 21 or all 21? We put all 21.   [29:44] We put every single one of them in there in detail where it's located. How much capital it took, how long it took to build the project, the details of the project, what it's cash flowed since its existence, like year-over-year financials, links to individual financial models that these people can look at when they're doing their due diligence on month overmonth analytics for the financials, year-over-year analytics for the financials.   [30:10] Uh, pretty much every report that you could generate out of a QuickBooks accounting software, we would put inside of this document linked into a Google sheet that an individual could look at and do their due diligence with.   [30:23] This in itself was a huge I'd say once again about a 10% increase in trust type action based on going above and beyond in the deck. Okay.   [30:33] Once again, what we're trying to do when I talk about some of these variables is we are attempting to avoid the surface level logic. Okay, surface level logic would be just like send them some information.   [30:45] you know, surface level things that could be a 10% action that end up getting reduced to a one to two percent action are well, you know, send them over like some some some information like a bio about the investors, you know, like send them over like their LinkedIn profile.   [31:01] It's like, well, that's not what somebody really gives a fuck about when they're doing due diligence and trying to increase their trust on somebody they don't know to give $750,000 to to invest into a car wash, is it?   [31:15] They want to know that this person's going to do the right thing when something goes wrong. This this individual who's considering investing, they want to know that you're putting yourself out there like you're not trying to hide anything about yourself or that it's even close to being interpreted that you're trying to hide something about yourself.   [31:34] They want to know that you are fucking transparent, that you've got their back. Okay? They want to know if something goes wrong, what are you going to do?   [31:43] And so those are the kind of stories that we would tell. Those are the kind of informative documentations and and the transparency that we would put into this.   [31:52] So to be clear in this particular case, 151 out of 152 people had successfully reinvested into future projects with this company. So there really wasn't an instance where we could demonstrate what would happen when something goes wrong.   [32:06]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Talking About Losers"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados numericamente   VISUAL:  Estratégia de transparência: falar sobre falhas, clientes que não tiveram sucesso, construir confiança através de honestidade   [32:08] But I'll give you an example with our agency as an example. [33:24] And anyway, long story short, like I said, I'm not too sure on specifically what he was raising capital for. It might have been multif family. It might have been commercial.   [33:33] But anyway, we uh we get everything we need from Charles. We get him to shoot some ads. We get him to shoot a VSSL. We get everything set up for him. We get them launched. Leads are rolling in. He's getting qualified people who are accredited.   [33:48] I don't remember the investment range that he needed, but long story short, we were getting a lot like a lot of qualified leads, per usual.   [33:57] Now, here's the issue. Charles had his fucking secretary just reaching out to people like a dentist office just hey wanted to touch base you know you fill out this form like you are you interested in investing with us they would do nothing literally not a single thing that I'm talking to you about here they wouldn't reveal their rolodex of historical investors they wouldn't reveal like a lot of historical project details because they just weren't willing to put in the work you know they weren't willing to create a lot of these sales assets that build trust dramatically faster They weren't willing to create like specific follow-up videos.   [34:34] They weren't willing to interview past clients and customers and investors. Um, they weren't even willing to go to different job sites that they were in the middle of doing or historically have completed and like once again just create any kind of videos.   [34:49] And on top of that, he was not even willing to take calls himself. He was essentially having his secretary like set calls for him and his secretary was not a good salesperson.   [35:00] Whenever we talk to somebody who is wanting to raise capital, I use Charles as a very transparent failure where I say, "Yeah, so listen like you want to raise capital like let me give you some examples where it did work."   [35:14] And we have a lot of those examples, you know, just as we do in any niche that we operate in. We have a lot of tremendously great case studies, but at the same time, you know, we got our losers, too.   [35:25] We talk about 5050. We talk about 50% of our winners for what goes right and what made it go right. But at the same time, when it comes to expectations and also creating trust, we talk about our losers just as much.   [35:39] We talk in detail about what made it not work. And we're not irrational about it either. If we made it fail, we talk about the fact that we made it fail in a particular way.   [35:50] In this case, in Charles's instance, we did a great job. He fucked it up by not doing things that we needed him to do. He also fucked it up by not having the right salespeople on the phone on the other side of it.   [36:03] But to be fair, you know, regardless, we still gave Charles his money back, you know.   [36:08] So my point is is like we still went out of our way to make it right. An individual who is sitting there, we are talking on the phone with potentially about to close to help ideally become a winning case study of ours, a great client. We want them to hear both sides.   [36:25] Not just from the perspective of building trust, but also from the perspective of setting the right expectations, but also from the perspective mainly in this case of trust.   [36:35] I want them to understand like look, something goes wrong, we do the right thing. Okay? If something goes wrong and it's our fault, we do the right thing.   [36:45] If something goes wrong and it's your fault, like here's what we're going to try to do to help. Once again, we won't even do the deal in the instance that you're this type of person because we've learned that that type of person, they don't do what they need to do to get successful in the first place. So, we don't want to do that deal. There's a high risk of failure.   [37:05] You know, that helps us condense our sales cycle. Okay? All that helps us once again get deals sooner.   [37:12] So, to be clear, ask yourself a simple question. How often are your salespeople talking about losers?   [37:19]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "50/50 Winner/Loser Strategy"   NÚMEROS:  50% winners, 50% losers discussed   VISUAL:  Estratégia balanceada: falar igualmente sobre sucessos e fracassos, evitar "too good to be true"   [37:21] We had a inner circle student recently, uh what's his name? Uh Jordan, and to be clear, they were going through a process where they asked a very simple question. They were like, "Dude, our salespeople are giving weird feedback where people are saying things like it sounds too good to be true and our closing rate is like dropping as a result of it."   [37:42] And I asked him the same question. I was like, "Well, how how often are you talking about your losers?" You know, like I guarantee you've had people buy your program that have just done terrible with it, you know, that you potentially needed to refund or that have just bitched and whined and like not got a good result and maybe even not even logged in.   [38:01] You like, how often do you talk about those people? And they brought their head of sales onto the call and their head of sales was like, "Yeah, dude. We don't talk about that at all."   [38:10] Like, wait, we're literally only talking about the people who have done tremendously well with it. Okay.   [38:16] So, to be clear, not Yeah. Jord Jacob. Yeah. So to be clear, it's like, dude, you got to talk about your losers just as much as you talk about the winners. Why? Because it builds trust. Okay?   [38:30] When we only talk about winners, it does not sound good. It sounds too good to be true. And when things are too good to be true, that equals low trust.   [38:40] when things have a realitybased perspective that we can take on the situation. Like as an example with the car wash people again in that case study, they've had projects that they've been able to build successfully in like months.   [38:54] You know, they get the amount of capital they need. They have the perfect construction companies. The weather's perfect because they're building a physical fucking car wash on planet Earth. Everything just goes super smooth. They immediately are fucking stuffed full of business. As soon as that car wash opens up, things are just great.   [39:12] In other instances, out of their 21 historical projects, they've had like four that have been delayed in their in their ability to get like a permit or their ability to build something based on fucked up weather for half the year or based on like a lack of ability to find employees in a specific area, you know?   [39:32] Or as an example, maybe it just takes 6 months to ramp up to close to full capacity on a day-over-day basis where it's making as much money as it can as a car wash where they then get the leverage to be able to start raising prices or introducing upselles, etc.   [39:48] In other instances, out of those 21 total projects, once again, four of them are kind of like not losers. I mean, they're still winners, but like they went slower. They had issues.   [39:59] The people had their capital tied up longer than two years. [40:17] We'd share the financials with them. Think about how incredible that is for a company that you're considering putting money into to be excessively transparent.   [40:27] What would an individual have to do if you didn't share your numbers like that? Well, with 21 different locations, first of all, they'd have to find all 21 locations under that brand. Okay?   [40:38] Second of all, they wouldn't have access to any of the financials. So our there's only I would submit   [53:48] a few situations where maybe we as a company knowingly dropped the ball on a client.   [53:55] But there's lots of cases of clients who don't freaking follow the process that we told them to follow. For example, to be successful, they got to be on two coaching calls per week for 12 weeks, and they got to make um uh four Marco Polo posts a week.   [54:13] And when we go through people who fail, have a have a reset, who drink during the 90 days, almost always, in fact, I would even say 100% of the time, it's because they didn't attend the two calls a week and they didn't make the Marco Apollo post four times a day. They just drift off. are kind of being very passive.   [54:34] So my question is, should we lead with where we as a company fucked up or should we lead where our clients fucked up because they didn't do what we told them to do?   [54:45] Yeah. So we we do it two ways. So how how we'll present it is very simple. We'll just say, "Look, our program's the shit We want to cover all of the details about why it's the shit and why it's going to work for you. [55:16] That way you can see very transparently what type of person you need to be in order to get this thing to work. Okay? And also where we've historically dropped the ball and where we've revamped our process to further increase our success rate. Let me get started with a few that haven't worked. Okay?   [55:34] And sometimes there's opportunities to just kind of work them in where you know like as I said like we're talking to some people about you know I I can I I can simply put as a salesperson at this point be self-aware like if I am in the middle of a conversation on a sales call and I have highlighted like three things that have worked well or I'm like only talking about the things that work well I'll be self-aware and I'll interject something that like hasn't worked you know just to balance out the conversation from that trust perspective.   [56:05] So you don't necessarily always have to lead with it, but you have to make sure you work it in no matter what. You have to make sure that you like sew in some opportunities to just talk about things that haven't worked.   [56:17] And it follows the logic of once again like who it's for and who it's not for because yeah, to your point like if somebody sits there on the call and they say I'm not the type of person that's going to be able to post twice a week, you know, or I'm not the type of person that's going to be able to do what you're asking me to do.   [56:35] Well, they simply put right away they know that they're probable to fail with it and or they right away they know, okay, this is going to work for me, you know, like that's pretty simple. You just don't want to finish the sales call without being able to talk about some losers. Simply put.   [57:04] Yeah. Okay, cool. Got it. Thank you very much.   [57:07] Yeah, no problem. Yeah. [57:42] Like, for whatever reason, these guys end up accidentally saying the wrong thing. like because they know they're not they're not trained like our closers. [58:15] So to your point, you just kind of want to monitor it. You know, if you see like a massive flux in your statistics where after you implement that specific trust asset, your closing ratio goes down or like your show rates fuck then yeah, you know that there's likely somebody on that list that has to get removed off of it that's fucking up your flow.   [58:36] Um, I think that the probability is far greater that it has a positive reaction rather than an adverse reaction. [01:00:38] You know, I really want to go out of my way here to try to think what is this person looking for that would help them trust me faster.   [01:00:47] And when I think of it from that perspective, you unlock a tremendous amount of additional content ideas, email marketing ideas, salesperson follow-up or salesperson conversation ideas, sales asset ideas that you otherwise just aren't thinking with when you're not isolating the thoughts to trust. [01:05:02]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "High-Ticket Sales Example: Custom Home"   NÚMEROS:  $2.5M project, $400K down payment, $100K monthly, 80K profit on land   VISUAL:  Exemplo prático de venda high-ticket: barndominium de 12,000 sq ft, processo de due diligence, transparência total do vendedor   [01:05:03] Anyway, long story short, I was going to build this 12,000 square foot like big ass barnaminium on it, you know, like this because it's in this farmland in Texas. It was going to look good, match the style, and it was going to have an indoor basketball court.   [01:05:18] Now, these people wanted me to put $2.5 million into this project altogether. And to be clear, they were actually pretty cool about how I was going to be able to pay for it.   [01:05:28] Rather than just having to lump sum 2.5 mil over to them, which I definitely wouldn't do. Um, they told me that I could essentially like finance it with them.   [01:05:38] So without any interest, I could just like lump sum different parts of the project at different stages, which is very common and it's it's known as developer financing.   [01:05:48] So like as an example, if there's a new building being developed in Miami, like some skyscraper, you don't have to go to a bank. the developer themselves will be like, "All right, yeah, put 30% down and then when we reach this milestone, you put another 10% down and then like, etc. for, you know, until you're 100% paid and the building's actually ready for you to move in."   [01:06:09] These people were going to do it the same. They were like, "Yeah, you'll put like, you know, 400k down and then you could do like a 100k a month, you know, because that's going to be the amount of time it probably takes to complete it anyway."   [01:06:22] where they were like, "Or you could put like this amount down and then when we complete this, you could put like this amount down, you know, just the same logic as the developer financing."   [01:06:31] And I was highly considering doing it, you know. I was like, "Yeah, sure." I like, "Do you do you have anybody else that's like built a project of similar size? You know, I'd love to talk to somebody that's had one of these homes for a few years and has lived in it. You know, I want to get their perspective."   [01:06:47] And they were like, "Yeah, yeah, sure." And they sent me over four people that had built homes that were like, I think the biggest one was maybe 9,000 square feet. the smallest one was maybe 6,000 square feet, which we're still pretty pretty like big homes relative to relative to a barnuminium.   [01:07:05] And I just had a bunch of questions for him, you know, because I don't know anybody that lives in a fucking barnuminium. And it's a new style of home and it's like very like open concept, which which I was interested in.   [01:07:17] And you know, it's also like the aesthetic of a fucking barn, you know? So, I wanted to I wanted to figure it out.   [01:07:23] So, I had all kinds of questions like about AC, about like how it sounds in the house when it's raining since it's a metal roof. um about just the general like electrical efficiency of the home, like whether there were problems or not with the property and just the questions about the builder, you know, like did they do a good job, were they dicks, were they cool, like what what did they do when things went wrong, you know?   [01:07:48] And any I talked to all four of these people and all four of these people were very like transparent. They were just homeowners that had used this builder and they were like, "Yeah, know these guys are great. Like they're really cool. Uh there were like no emotion the entire time. If something would go wrong, it was mostly like weather."   [01:08:07] Um, some of these people had actually built during like extreme hikes in prices of material goods and they were like, "Yeah, they actually somehow I I don't know if they like pre-bought material or something, but they were actually still able to honor the original pricing that they quoted me and they didn't have to increase the the cost of the project, which was fucking sweet."   [01:08:28] And every single one of them said the same thing. They were like, the only thing I the only thing that kind of caught me off was like they finished it far sooner than what I expected.   [01:08:37] So, you know, three out of four of these people also did the developer financing. One of them did cash. So, the one who did cash was like, "Yeah, that was really good."   [01:08:46] But the three out of four that did the developer financing, they were like, I mean, it was ready to move in like six or seven months prior to me being like, you know, on the developer financing schedule.   [01:08:57] So, like I didn't have a budget to just randomly give them the remaining six months worth of costs just because it got done, you know, and they weren't mad about that or anything. They just kept me on the same schedule and I just moved into it a little later.   [01:09:10] But yeah, they were like that actually ended up benefiting me, you know, cuz like, you know, it was much better to know it was done rather than just like I was literally moving into it the days after it got completed.   [01:09:22] And I didn't I didn't pay anything to fuck talk to any of those people. And like, no joke, right after I got done talking to those people, I was excessively sold on the logic of doing this and moving forward on the project, uh, I then went and just made a final decision based on visiting Houston again.   [01:09:42] And so when I visited Houston, I went during the middle of summertime and I just I I consistently monitored the weather, but you know, after I had boughten the the land, I had a bunch of people make like really weird comments about Houston's weather and they just kept saying how it was like the armpit of Texas was a very common one I'd hear.   [01:10:03] Um, one of the inner circle students, Sean, told me that he grew up in Houston and he had a heat stroke when he was a kid. Uh, just made me question like how hot it actually was out there during the summer.   [01:10:15] So, I went and I was like, dude, somehow it's hotter than Miami, you know, like, fuck this. Uh, so anyway, I determined just based on the weather alone, I didn't want to I didn't want to live in this area, you know, and I didn't want to move there. So, like I said, I ended up selling the land.   [01:10:32] But my my point is regardless, you know, the people who are selling something for fucking literally $2.5 million, they're willing to let me talk to a buyer without having to put a fucking deposit down or like show them. They know that I'm serious. That's why I'm fucking trying to talk to the buyers, you know?   [01:10:50] gotta be fucking retarded to think I'm trying to talk to historical buyers without actually being ser like why would I talk to the buyers you know if if to be clear I'm like a secret shopper you know and I'm somebody who works with like a competing company you know and I'm lying about my name or something like that uh sure you know sure you could you could assume that like that would be an example of the downside and why you might want somebody to put a deposit down but like dude literally what's the probability of that in comparison to just the probability that they're like me and an actual serious buyer and just wanted to talk to people to sell themselves more.   [01:11:27] There's a very common fallacy I find in sales which is like thinking that people want to find ways to not trust you where generally it's the opposite.   [01:11:37] If you just kind of are self-aware and you look at what types of questions people ask and like what people do during a sales process, a majority of the time, even in your own sales processes that you go through as buyers, you are looking for reasons to buy rather than looking for reasons not to buy.   [01:11:55] You get what I mean? Like if you were looking for reasons not to buy, you're generally sold already and you're trying to unsell yourself versus once again the opposite is true which is okay like I'm curious and I want to do this but like I'm looking for information that greases the wheels on my decision becoming easier like help me trust you you know help me like make the sale happen.   [01:12:17] And when a salesperson thinks from that perspective, which is which is what I'm thinking with, if I hear you say like, "Dude, I have to give you a deposit to be able to talk to people." That's just unnecessary friction. So, I have a slightly different style of mastermind I plan on doing this one if the attendance is a little lighter where we get a little more like one-on-one stuff going and like smaller group stuff going. So, it'll be pretty cool.   [01:24:46] If you're into more like intimate masterminds in comparison to some of them where the room is like fucking maxed out, you know, and there's like 60 people there.   [01:24:55] So, if uh if you're into that kind of stuff, make sure you come to the uh July 20th and 21st Q3 mastermind coming up here. Excited to see you guys, per usual. And yeah, looking forward to it. Talk soon, guys. Call. Thank you. Bye.     📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO   NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS: Sales cycle reduzido de  9-16 semanas  para  3 semanas Investimento mínimo:  $75,000 Investimento máximo:  $1,000,000 26%  dos leads com capacidade de $700K-$1M Retorno cash-on-cash:  28% em menos de 2 anos 152 investidores históricos  totais 151 de 152  reinvestiram (taxa de  99.3% ) 90+  investidores concordaram em estar no rolodex 20+  vídeos de case studies criados Taxa de play dos vídeos:  15% 21 projetos históricos  de car wash documentados 4 de 21  projetos tiveram delays/problemas 6 investidores  dispostos a fazer calls com prospects 30+ clientes  (exemplo do James) dispostos a falar sem incentivo 33 reviews  no Google (exemplo do James) Proporção recomendada de conteúdo:  60% sucessos / 40% fracassos   ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:   Speed to Trust Framework Equação: Alta confiança = Alta velocidade Baixa confiança = Ciclo lento Reframe: "Cold leads" = "Low trust leads"   High Impact vs Low Impact Actions Ações de 1-2% trust: case studies, emails básicos, follow-ups padrão Ações de 10-20% trust: rolodex de investidores, transparência total, falar sobre fracassos   West Coast Social Vetting Strategy Rolodex de clientes históricos (nome, localização, projeto) Sem informações de contato (reduzir fricção) Permitir que prospects encontrem conexões mútuas Law of association para transferência de confiança   The Research Deck (Due Diligence Asset) Todos os 21 projetos históricos documentados Financials completos (QuickBooks) linkados Background checks dos fundadores Links para redes sociais Fotos com famílias (não apenas profissionais) Detalhes de projetos que falharam/atrasaram   50/50 Winner/Loser Transparency Falar igualmente sobre sucessos e fracassos Evitar "too good to be true" Estabelecer expectativas realistas Demonstrar integridade quando algo dá errado Exemplo Charles: caso de fracasso usado como learning   Multiple Trust Asset Formats Mesma informação em formatos diferentes Google Reviews + Página dedicada com links Grupo de Facebook + Calls 1-on-1 Testimonials escritos + Vídeo calls com clientes   Friction Reduction Strategy Não exigir depósito para falar com clientes Não forçar opt-ins desnecessários Facilitar acesso à informação Schedule follow-up calls ao invés de criar barreiras   ESTRUTURA:   Pre-Call Assets: Rolodex de investidores históricos Research deck com 21 projetos Case study videos (20+) Email sequences com transparência   On-Call Strategy: CFO fazendo as calls (não secretária) Perguntar: "O que falta para você confiar?" Trabalhar fracassos na conversa Balancear 60% wins / 40% losses   Post-Call Assets: Follow-up emails Conexão por texto com clientes históricos Schedule de próxima call Vídeos adicionais se necessário   DADOS FINANCEIROS: Projeto car wash:  $2-5M  por localização Retorno:  28% cash-on-cash  em <24 meses Beats S&P:  2-3% ao ano  acima do mercado Exemplo conversão:  $1M → $1.28M  em 2 anos Exemplo barndominium:  $2.5M  projeto Down payment:  $400K Parcelas:  $100K/mês Lucro na venda do terreno Houston:  $80K   MÉTRICAS DE CONFIANÇA: Rolodex impact:  10% trust increase Research deck impact:  10% trust increase Case studies impact:  1-2% trust increase  (baixo play rate) Transparência sobre fracassos:  Crítico  para evitar "too good to be true"   DURAÇÃO:  1h 24min  FORMATO:  Group coaching call / Case study presentation
